questions.q: In an undirected graph, what is an edge?
options: A directed connection from one vertex to another
A bidirectional connection between two vertices
A connection from a vertex to itself
A weighted path between multiple vertices
questions.explanation: In an undirected graph, an edge connects two vertices bidirectionally (symmetric). In a directed graph, edges are one-way (asymmetric). A self-loop is an edge from a vertex to itself.

questions.q: What is the adjacency matrix representation of a graph?
options: A linked list of vertices and their neighbors
A 2D array where entry [i][j] indicates the connection between vertices i and j
A list of edges sorted by weight
A tree structure representing vertex relationships
questions.explanation: An adjacency matrix is an n×n matrix where entry [i][j] is non-zero (typically 1 or the edge weight) if there's an edge from i to j. It's space O(n²) but enables O(1) edge lookups.

questions.q: What is an adjacency list representation of a graph?
options: A matrix of binary values indicating connectivity
An array of lists where each vertex stores its neighboring vertices
A sorted list of all edges in the graph
A queue of unvisited vertices
questions.explanation: An adjacency list stores for each vertex a list of its adjacent vertices. It's space O(V + E), more efficient than matrices for sparse graphs. Edge lookup is O(degree) rather than O(1).

questions.q: In a directed graph, what is an in-degree of a vertex?
options: The number of outgoing edges from the vertex
The number of incoming edges to the vertex
The total number of edges in the graph
The number of vertices the graph contains
questions.explanation: In-degree is the count of edges entering a vertex. Out-degree is the count of edges leaving a vertex. In an undirected graph, both are the same (the degree).

questions.q: What is a strongly connected component in a directed graph?
options: A subgraph where every vertex can reach every other vertex
A vertex with in-degree and out-degree both greater than 1
Any connected subgraph of the overall graph
A cycle that includes all vertices
questions.explanation: A strongly connected component (SCC) is a maximal set of vertices where every vertex is reachable from every other vertex through directed paths. SCCs are important for analyzing graph structure.

options: Arranging vertices in a circular order
Ordering vertices such that for every directed edge u → v, u comes before v in the ordering
Sorting vertices by their degree values
Arranging vertices to minimize the sum of edge weights
questions.explanation: A topological sort is a linear ordering of vertices in a directed acyclic graph (DAG) such that edges always go from earlier to later vertices. It's useful for task scheduling and dependency resolution.

questions.q: In breadth-first search (BFS), which data structure is typically used?
options: A stack
A priority queue
A queue
A binary tree
questions.explanation: BFS uses a queue (FIFO) to explore vertices level by level. Depth-first search (DFS) uses a stack (LIFO) or recursion. Priority queues are used in Dijkstra's algorithm.

questions.q: What is the time complexity of depth-first search on a graph with V vertices and E edges?
options: O(V)
O(E)
O(V + E)
O(V × E)
questions.explanation: DFS visits each vertex once (O(V)) and explores each edge once (O(E)), resulting in O(V + E) total time. This applies to both adjacency list and matrix representations, though constants differ.

questions.q: What is a cycle in a graph?
options: A path from a vertex to itself
A path that visits every vertex exactly once
A subgraph with more edges than vertices
Any closed path in the graph
questions.explanation: A cycle is a closed path where the starting vertex is reached again. The minimum cycle has 3 vertices (in an undirected graph without multi-edges). A path visiting all vertices is a Hamiltonian path, different from a cycle.

questions.q: What is the space complexity of adjacency list representation for a graph with V vertices and E edges?
options: O(V²)
O(V + E)
O(E)
O(V × E)
questions.explanation: An adjacency list stores V vertices and each edge appears once (or twice for undirected graphs), requiring O(V + E) space. An adjacency matrix always uses O(V²) space regardless of edge count.
